docs.text: # FOURIA FL Studio Production Notes These are original starter notes for FOURIA's local RAG memory. Add your own beat breakdowns, MIDI exports, channel presets, prompt/response pairs, and FL workflows here over time. ## Fast FL Studio Loop Workflow 1. Set tempo and key before sound selection. 2. Build an 8-bar loop with chords, melody, drums, 808, and one ear-candy sound. 3. Route every major sound to the mixer immediately: drums, 808, melody, counter, vocals, and effects. 4. Gain-stage before mixing. Leave the master below clipping and keep the 808 controlled before adding loudness. 5. Duplicate the loop into arrangement: intro, hook, verse, hook, bridge or break, final hook, outro. ## Piano Roll Use scale highlighting to stay in key, then break the grid only after the idea is clear. Use Alt+R for velocity variation and Alt+S for strum. Keep low notes away from the 808 unless they intentionally support the root. ## Chords Dark trap in minor keys often works with i-VI-III-VII, i-iv-VI-v, i-VII-VI-VII, and i-III-VII-VI. For F minor, useful chords include Fm, Db, Ab, Eb, Bbm, and Cm. Keep chord voicings simple, then add tension with top-note movement, octave doubles, passing notes, and sparse counter melodies. ## Melody Strong melodies use repetition plus one small change. Start with the root, minor third, fifth, sixth, or seventh. Use call-and-response between the main melody an

lodies. ## Melody Strong melodies use repetition plus one small change. Start with the root, minor third, fifth, sixth, or seventh. Use call-and-response between the main melody and counter melody. Avoid filling every beat; leave space for drums and vocals. ## Drums Kick and clap/snare define the bounce. Closed hats can carry motion, but velocity and rests matter more than constant rolls. Add open hats and percs at phrase ends or before drops. Make the first drum pass simple enough that the 808 pattern can breathe. ## 808 Tune the 808 to the song key and make it monophonic unless glide is intentional. Follow chord roots first, then add passing notes. If the low end is muddy, shorten 808 notes, remove low melody notes, reduce overlap with kick, and use saturation instead of just turning the 808 up. ## Mixing Clean production starts with levels. EQ is for removing conflicts before boosting character. High-pass non-bass instruments when needed, but do not thin everything out automatically. Use reference tracks for balance, not for copying every sound. ## Exporting For Critique Export MIDI for notes, timing, and harmony critique. Export stems named by role: kick, 808, snare, hats, melody, counter, vocal, fx. Include BPM and key in the folder name when possible.
fx. Include BPM and key in the folder name when possible.

# FOURIA Production Knowledge Core ## Production method Clarify or infer genre, subgenre, BPM, key, scale, mood, section length, and requested instrument. Build in layers: harmonic foundation, main motif, rhythm section, bass/808, counterpoint, transitions, arrangement, mix, then master. Every layer needs a role. Remove parts that compete for rhythm, register, or attention. Save headroom and verify actions inside FL Studio. ## FL Studio workflow Use Channel Rack for generators and patterns, Piano Roll for note programming, Playlist for arrangement, Mixer for routing and processing, Browser for assets, automation clips for movement, Patcher for reusable chains, Edison for editing, NewTone for pitch/time work, and reference tracks routed separately from the master processing chain. Name, color, route, gain-stage, and organize channels. Use ghost notes, scale highlighting, note properties, strum, arpeggiation, velocity, swing, and controlled timing variation deliberately. ## Harmony and melody Choose a scale appropriate to the mood. Establish chord function, voice-lead between nearby notes, avoid unnecessary low-register chord mud, and reserve space for bass. Strong melodies combine motif repetition, rhythmic identity, contour, rests, tension notes, and phrase-end resolution. Countermelodies answer rather than duplicate the lead. ## Drums and bass Kick and bass must cooperate in p

thmic identity, contour, rests, tension notes, and phrase-end resolution. Countermelodies answer rather than duplicate the lead. ## Drums and bass Kick and bass must cooperate in pitch, timing, envelope, and frequency. Tune 808s, control note overlap, use slides intentionally, and write bass rhythm around the kick. Groove comes from placement, velocity, sound length, swing, syncopation, silence, and call-and-response—not random complexity. ## Arrangement Create contrast through subtraction, register, density, automation, fills, transitions, harmonic change, and new countermelodies. Typical sections include intro, verse/build, hook/drop, variation, bridge/breakdown, final hook/drop, and outro, but genre and song intent decide the form. ## Mixing Start with balance and gain staging. Correct source and arrangement problems before processing. Use EQ for conflicts, compression for dynamics/envelope, saturation for density, transient shaping for attack/sustain, reverb and delay for depth, and automation for musical movement. Check mono, low volume, headphones, speakers, spectrum, phase, clipping, and reference translation. ## Mastering Mastering cannot repair a broken mix. Preserve transients and tonal balance, control peaks, make small broad corrections, compare loudness-matched references, and choose final loudness for the genre and delivery platform. Avoid chasing loudness at the

nce, control peaks, make small broad corrections, compare loudness-matched references, and choose final loudness for the genre and delivery platform. Avoid chasing loudness at the expense of punch, depth, or distortion. ## Genre grammar - Trap: 120–160 BPM commonly felt half-time; sparse minor harmony, agile hats, hard snare/clap backbeat, tuned 808s, negative space, short motifs. - Drill: sliding bass, dark modes, syncopated kick, sparse snare placement, tense bells/strings/pianos, abrupt rhythmic gaps. - Boom bap: sampled or sample-like harmony, swung drums, strong snare, bass pocket, chops, texture, and human timing. - R&B/neo-soul: extended chords, voice leading, pocket, warm bass, expressive melody, restrained drums, tasteful chromatic approach notes. - Pop: immediate hook, clear structure, controlled density, strong transitions, layered vocals/melodies, polished low end and top end. - House/techno: four-on-floor foundation, bass-kick relationship, evolving automation, loop variation, tension/release, DJ-friendly phrasing. - Amapiano: log-drum conversation, deep groove, shakers/percussion, spacious harmony, long-form development, restrained melodic hooks. - Afrobeats/dancehall/reggaeton/dembow: interlocking percussion, syncopated bass, concise hooks, rhythmic guitars/keys, vocal space, genre-specific clave and kick/snare relationships. - DnB/jungle: break manipulation, fas

nterlocking percussion, syncopated bass, concise hooks, rhythmic guitars/keys, vocal space, genre-specific clave and kick/snare relationships. - DnB/jungle: break manipulation, fast tempo, sub control, syncopation, pads or stabs, energetic phrase edits. - Cinematic/orchestral: motif, orchestration by register and function, dynamics, articulation, harmonic pacing, and narrative development. ## Command policy For “make/build/add” requests, produce an executable plan and perform only supported verified actions. Report generated key, scale, BPM, notes, channels, plugins, mixer routing, and files. If FL automation is uncertain, say where it stopped instead of claiming success.

# FOURIA Open Production Curriculum Sources This file is a source registry. FOURIA uses distilled notes and retains attribution. It does not claim that open educational material alone verifies an automated FL Studio action. ## Mixing and Mastering — Wikibooks - Source: https://en.wikibooks.org/wiki/Mixing_and_Mastering - License: CC BY-SA (Wikibooks) - Curriculum: source selection, balance, bass mixdown, dynamics, spatial processing, monitoring, and mastering workflow. - Core lesson: solve arrangement and source problems before reaching for EQ; low-end clarity begins with compatible sounds, register, and timing. ## Sound Synthesis Theory — Wikibooks - Source: https://en.wikibooks.org/wiki/Sound_Synthesis_Theory - License: CC BY-SA (Wikibooks) - Curriculum: oscillators, spectra, envelopes, modulation, subtractive and additive concepts, and timbral design. - Application: translate synthesis concepts into FL generators and third-party plugins without tying knowledge to a single interface. ## Sound Recording — Wikibooks - Source: https://en.wikibooks.org/wiki/Sound_Recording - License: CC BY-SA (Wikibooks) - Curriculum: signal flow, recording levels, microphones, monitoring, and editing fundamentals. ## Open Music Theory - Source: https://viva.pressbooks.pub/openmusictheory/ - Catalog: https://open.umn.edu/opentextbooks/textbooks/1254 - License: open educational resource; verify ch

pen Music Theory - Source: https://viva.pressbooks.pub/openmusictheory/ - Catalog: https://open.umn.edu/opentextbooks/textbooks/1254 - License: open educational resource; verify chapter attribution when importing. - Curriculum: fundamentals, rhythm, meter, diatonic/chromatic harmony, form, counterpoint, jazz, popular music, and orchestration. - Application: chord function, voice leading, phrase design, harmonic rhythm, tension/release, formal contrast, and genre-aware composition. ## Learning policy 1. Store source URL and license with every imported lesson. 2. Prefer distilled operational rules and exercises over copied prose. 3. Separate universal audio principles from genre taste. 4. Treat loudness, EQ frequencies, compressor settings, and plugin chains as context-dependent starting points—not laws. 5. Mark FL actions verified only after observable project-state evidence.

# FOURIA Corpus Put allowed local reference material here: - your own FL Studio notes - your own beat breakdowns - your own plugin-chain recipes - open/licensed music theory notes - exported production checklists Use RAG for third-party manuals and copyrighted material unless you have explicit permission to train on it. ## YouTube lessons `scripts/ingest_youtube.py` accepts a video, playlist, or channel URL. It temporarily downloads available English captions, generates original attributed study notes with the local FOURIA model, and deletes the raw captions. Check the creator's license/terms and only ingest material you are allowed to use. The script strictly rejects videos unless metadata explicitly reports Creative Commons, CC BY, CC0, or public-domain licensing.
